questions.scenario: At 10:20 UTC, the team discovers a bad release started corrupting order statuses at 10:12 UTC. The business says: `checkout must be safe for customers again within 30 minutes.` Which target is this?

options.text: RTO, because it describes how long recovery may take before the impact is unacceptable.
RPO, because it describes how much data may be missing after restore.
Retention, because it describes how long logs stay in CloudWatch.
Desired count, because it describes how many ECS tasks should run.

questions.explanation: RTO is the recovery time objective. It answers how quickly a usable service path should come back. It does not say which restore point to use or how much data loss is acceptable.

questions.scenario: For the same incident, the business says: `after recovery, we can reconcile a few minutes of accepted orders, but we cannot lose an hour.` Which target is this?

options.text: RPO, because it describes acceptable data loss or data freshness after recovery.
RTO, because it describes how fast the app process starts.
ALB idle timeout, because it controls customer data loss.
CPU reservation, because it controls how fresh restored rows are.

questions.explanation: RPO is about the recovery point and how much recent data the business can afford to lose or re-create. It is separate from how long recovery takes. A fast restore to old data can have good RTO and poor RPO.

questions.scenario: The only confirmed backup is a nightly snapshot from 02:00 UTC. The bad write starts at 10:12 UTC, and the business accepts at most 5 minutes of order data loss.

options.text: The backup schedule probably cannot meet the RPO for accepted orders.
The RTO is automatically met because any backup exists.
The RPO is automatically zero because the snapshot is from the same day.
Backups are unrelated to data loss once the app is rolled back.

questions.explanation: A nightly snapshot may be far too old for a 5-minute RPO. The team may need point-in-time recovery, repair from side restore, or other data protection. Backup existence is not the same as meeting the target.

questions.scenario: Which statements are true for the orders recovery plan?

options.text: RTO depends on restore speed, validation, app config, traffic switch, and people who can run the steps.
RPO depends on backup frequency, point-in-time restore capability, and how much valid data can be reconciled.
Restore point choice can remove bad writes but may also exclude good orders created later.
RTO and RPO become guaranteed as soon as they are written in a document.

questions.explanation: RTO and RPO are promises that need tested systems and practiced people behind them. Restore point choice is a data decision, not only an AWS action. A document alone does not make the targets real.

questions.scenario: A proposal sets `RTO: 5 minutes` and `RPO: near zero` for every internal and customer-facing path. There is no extra budget, no automation plan, and no restore drill evidence.

options.text: Ask what cost, automation, backup, validation, and staffing changes would make those targets believable.
Accept the targets because lower numbers always make recovery cheaper.
Remove backups because aggressive targets mean the team will not need them.
Use the same target for every workload even if the business impact differs.

questions.explanation: Tighter targets usually cost more and require more preparation. Some paths deserve strong targets, but every target should match business value and tested capability. Otherwise the numbers are wishes.
